A1.example: She felt happy when she got the gift.
The food in this restaurant is delicious.

A1.definition: a person you like and enjoy being with
A1.example: My best friend lives next door.

A1.definition: the early part of the day
A1.example: I go for a walk every morning.

A2.example: Traveling to a new country is always an adventure.
A2.etymology: Latin adventura ‚ÄĒ things about to happen

A2.example: She wants to improve her English skills.
Can you explain this word to me?
The test was hard. However, I passed it.
I recently started a new course.

B1.example: Good communication is essential in any relationship.
B1.academic_note: Academic Word List ‚ÄĒ used at Harvard in essay criteria

B1.example: This job is a great opportunity for career growth.
B1.academic_note: Key word in business and social sciences

B1.example: You should consider all your options before deciding.
B1.etymology: Latin considerare ‚ÄĒ to observe the stars
B1.academic_note: Critical thinking verb, Oxford Academic Word List

B1.example: She achieved her goal of running a marathon.
B1.academic_note: Goal-oriented verb, common in Stanford course objectives

B1.example: He went to work despite feeling ill.

B2.definition: able to recover quickly from difficulties
B2.example: She's incredibly resilient ‚ÄĒ she bounced back after losing her job.
B2.academic_note: Psychology term popularised by Harvard studies on grit

B2.example: Change is inevitable in any growing company.
B2.academic_note: Philosophical term, used in Socratic dialogues on fate

B2.definition: to develop in detail; or detailed and complex
B2.example: Could you elaborate on your proposal?
B2.academic_note: Academic verb for expanding arguments, Oxford AWL

B2.example: Constant criticism can undermine someone's confidence.
B2.etymology: Medieval ‚ÄĒ to dig beneath a wall
B2.academic_note: Critical analysis vocabulary, common in Stanford political science

B2.definition: able to be maintained over time without damage
B2.example: We need sustainable solutions to climate change.
B2.academic_note: Harvard Kennedy School key term in policy studies

B2.definition: open to more than one interpretation
B2.example: The professor's instructions were ambiguous, causing confusion.
B2.academic_note: Socrates used this concept in dialogues on truth

B2.example: A coherent argument is the foundation of good academic writing.
B2.academic_note: Core criterion in Harvard essay rubrics

B2.definition: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
B2.example: Academic integrity means never plagiarising work.
B2.academic_note: Stanford Honor Code centers on this principle

B2.definition: a situation with two difficult choices
B2.example: The classic prisoner's dilemma reveals much about human cooperation.
B2.academic_note: Used by Socrates in ethical thought experiments

B2.definition: a proposed explanation to be tested
B2.example: Scientists must form a hypothesis before conducting an experiment.
B2.academic_note: Fundamental scientific method term at all universities

C1.example: The new policy only exacerbated the housing crisis.
C1.academic_note: Common in Harvard Policy Review and medical research

C1.definition: dealing with things sensibly and practically
C1.example: We need a pragmatic approach to solve this issue.
Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society.
C1.academic_note: Stanford tech studies; Plato used the concept for Forms

C1.definition: a subtle difference in meaning or feeling
C1.example: There's an important nuance between 'affect' and 'effect'.
C1.etymology: French nuer ‚ÄĒ to show variations of colour
C1.academic_note: Harvard Rhetoric Department emphasises nuance in argumentation

C1.definition: fluent and persuasive in speaking or writing
C1.example: She gave an eloquent speech that moved the entire audience.
C1.academic_note: Socrates and Aristotle both studied eloquence (rhetoric)

C1.example: This discovery shifted the paradigm of modern physics.
C1.academic_note: Thomas Kuhn's 'paradigm shift' coined at Harvard

C1.definition: placing two contrasting things side by side
C1.example: The poem uses juxtaposition of life and death powerfully.
C1.etymology: Latin juxta ‚ÄĒ next to + positio ‚ÄĒ placing
C1.academic_note: Literary analysis term at Oxford and Cambridge

C1.definition: to make something less severe or serious
C1.example: Exercise can mitigate the effects of stress.
C1.academic_note: Stanford Law and Harvard Medical School core vocabulary

C1.definition: a division between two opposite things
C1.example: The dichotomy between reason and emotion fascinated Plato.
C1.academic_note: Platonic philosophy ‚ÄĒ used in Socratic method of division

C1.definition: based on observation and experiment rather than theory
C1.example: Empirical evidence is the cornerstone of scientific research.
C1.academic_note: Foundation of Oxford's scientific method; Harvard research standards

C1.definition: effective and persuasive speaking or writing
C1.example: Political leaders rely on rhetoric to inspire their followers.
C1.etymology: Greek rhńďtorikńď ‚ÄĒ art of an orator
C1.academic_note: Aristotle's Rhetoric is still taught at Harvard and Stanford

C1.definition: relating to mental processes of learning and understanding
C1.example: Cognitive skills include memory, attention, and problem-solving.
C1.academic_note: Harvard cognitive science; Piaget's developmental theory

C2.example: Finding that rare manuscript in the archive was pure serendipity.
C2.etymology: Coined 1754 from Persian fairy tale 'The Three Princes of Serendip'
C2.academic_note: Used by Robert Merton (Columbia) in sociology of science

C2.definition: lasting for only a short time
C2.example: Fame can be ephemeral in the age of social media.
C2.academic_note: Plato's contrast between ephemeral world and eternal Forms

C2.definition: a person who flatters powerful people for personal gain
C2.example: Socrates criticised sycophants who praised tyrants to gain favour.
C2.academic_note: Socratic dialogues warn against sycophancy in democracy

C2.definition: generous and forgiving, especially towards rivals
C2.example: She was magnanimous in victory, publicly praising her opponent.
C2.academic_note: Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics describes magnanimity as a virtue

C2.definition: done with minimum effort and care; mechanical
C2.example: He gave a perfunctory nod and returned to his research.
C2.etymology: Latin perfunctus ‚ÄĒ to get through with
C2.academic_note: Academic peer review warns against perfunctory responses

C2.definition: calm composure, especially in difficult situations
C2.example: Stoic philosophers taught equanimity in the face of suffering.
C2.academic_note: Stoicism (Marcus Aurelius, Epictetus) ‚ÄĒ Harvard philosophy curriculum

C2.definition: having a ready insight into things; shrewd
C2.example: A perspicacious reader will notice the irony in the opening line.
C2.academic_note: Critical reading skill emphasised in Oxford tutorials

C2.definition: the view that only one's own mind is real
C2.example: Descartes' cogito ergo sum responds to the challenge of solipsism.
C2.academic_note: Core concept in Stanford Philosophy of Mind courses

C2.definition: the process of arriving at truth through reasoned argument
C2.example: The Socratic dialectic involves questioning assumptions to reveal truth.
C2.academic_note: Socrates' fundamental method; Hegel developed it further

C2.definition: a short, witty statement expressing a general truth
C2.example: 'Know thyself' is perhaps the most famous aphorism from ancient Greece.
C2.etymology: Greek aphorismos ‚ÄĒ definition, from horizein ‚ÄĒ to bound
C2.academic_note: Socratic philosophy; Harvard rhetoric courses study classical aphorisms

C2.definition: the appearance of being true or real
C2.example: A novelist must create verisimilitude to make readers believe the story.
C2.academic_note: Oxford literary criticism; Aristotle's Poetics on mimesis

C2.definition: relating to the nature of being and existence
C2.example: Descartes posed the ontological question: what does it mean to exist?
C2.academic_note: Stanford/Harvard philosophy; Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle's central concern
